chapter: Chapter: Electricity – Magnetic and Heating Effects

questions.question: Explain why a current-carrying conductor produces a magnetic field around it.
questions.answer: Moving electric charges create a magnetic field around the conductor. When current flows through a wire, the moving charges generate a magnetic field around it.

questions.question: Why does the filament of an electric bulb glow when current passes through it?
questions.answer: The filament offers resistance to the flow of current and gets heated to a very high temperature due to the heating effect of current, producing light.

questions.question: Explain why a fuse wire has a lower melting point than the connecting wires.
questions.answer: A fuse wire is made of a material with a low melting point so that it melts quickly when excessive current flows, breaking the circuit and protecting appliances.

questions.question: Why is soft iron preferred for making electromagnets instead of steel?
questions.answer: Soft iron becomes magnetized quickly when current flows and loses magnetism immediately when current stops, which is suitable for electromagnets.

questions.question: Explain why an electromagnet is considered a temporary magnet.
questions.answer: An electromagnet shows magnetism only when electric current flows through the coil. When the current stops, it loses its magnetism.

questions.question: Why are electric heaters designed with coils of high resistance material?
questions.answer: High resistance materials produce more heat when current passes through them, making them suitable for heating appliances.

questions.question: Explain how increasing the number of turns in a coil affects an electromagnet.
questions.answer: Increasing the number of turns increases the magnetic field strength, making the electromagnet stronger.

questions.question: Why does a compass needle deflect near a current-carrying wire?
questions.answer: The current-carrying wire produces a magnetic field that interacts with the compass needle, causing it to deflect.

questions.question: Explain why electric wires are made of copper but covered with plastic.
questions.answer: Copper allows electricity to flow easily because it is a good conductor, while plastic acts as an insulator to prevent electric shocks.

questions.question: Why can an electromagnet lift heavy iron objects but not wooden objects?
questions.answer: Electromagnets attract magnetic materials like iron, but non-magnetic materials such as wood are not attracted.

questions.question: Explain why the heating effect of current can sometimes be dangerous.
questions.answer: Excessive heating can melt wires, damage appliances, and cause electrical fires.

questions.question: Why are electric irons designed with thermostats?
questions.answer: Thermostats regulate temperature and prevent overheating of the iron.

questions.question: Explain how electric current can be used to produce magnetism.
questions.answer: When electric current flows through a coil of wire, it produces a magnetic field around the coil, creating an electromagnet.

questions.question: Why are safety devices necessary in electrical circuits?
questions.answer: Safety devices such as fuses prevent damage to appliances and reduce the risk of electric shocks or fires.

questions.question: Explain why thick wires are used in high-current electrical appliances.
questions.answer: Thicker wires can carry larger currents safely without overheating.

questions.question: Why does an electric crane use an electromagnet instead of a permanent magnet?
questions.answer: Electromagnets can be switched on and off easily, allowing objects to be picked up and released.

questions.question: Explain how increasing electric current affects the heating effect.
questions.answer: When electric current increases, the amount of heat produced in the conductor also increases.

questions.question: Why are electric appliances connected in parallel circuits at home?
questions.answer: Parallel circuits allow each appliance to receive the same voltage and operate independently.

questions.question: Explain why a damaged electric wire can cause a short circuit.
questions.answer: If the insulating layer breaks, wires may touch each other, allowing a large current to flow suddenly, causing a short circuit.

questions.question: Why is the heating effect of electricity useful in cooking appliances?
questions.answer: Cooking appliances use heat produced by electric current to cook food efficiently.

questions.question: Explain why increasing the current strengthens an electromagnet.
questions.answer: Higher current produces a stronger magnetic field around the coil, increasing the strength of the electromagnet.

questions.question: Why should electrical appliances not be handled with wet hands?
questions.answer: Water conducts electricity and increases the risk of electric shock.

questions.question: Explain how electric bells use the magnetic effect of current.
questions.answer: When current flows through the coil, it becomes an electromagnet and attracts an iron armature, producing a ringing sound.

questions.question: Why are insulating materials necessary in electrical wiring?
questions.answer: Insulating materials prevent current leakage and protect people from electric shocks.

questions.question: Explain why excessive current can damage electrical appliances.
questions.answer: Excessive current produces too much heat in wires and components, which can melt parts or cause electrical fires.
